I'm going to go ahead and assume that it didn't end up very well. The problem with win-win is that it's designed to be rational. And what we really deal with is emotional. Here's how all this evolved in hostage negotiation. When hostage negotiation was invented, they read business books. It was derived from bargaining. They thought that that approach would be most applicable. And you know what? It didn't work. Why? Because they're human beings that are driven by emotions. So when they realized that emotions were the issues, they got the tools for dealing with emotions, which are what we now refer to as emotional intelligence. So as we continued to evolve, and we had kidnapping negotiations, which were basically just bargaining situations, when we put emotional intelligence back into our bargaining, we gained the upper hand. I went to Harvard to study their negotiation and found that at that time they were heavy duty into what we used to call active listening skills, which is what we were doing. Completely different universe, applying the exact same ideas. Now also back in about 1980, a gentleman named Herb Cohen wrote a book on negotiations, and he said the negotiation was the use of information and power in a web of tension. This web of tension is emotions. And to think about it, many people like to think about negotiation as being analogous to playing chess. Here's the problem. The web of tension is as if the pieces on a chess board are connected by springs. Those are emotions. And each time you move one of the pieces, it changes the tension on all the other pieces, and they move. So with the recognition of how emotional intelligence affects bargaining, we created Never Split the Difference.
